artifact_text:
# inflection.py

A port of Ruby on Rails' string inflector to Python, providing functions to transform words between plural/singular forms and various identifier casing conventions.

## Public functions

**camelize(string, uppercase_first_letter=True)** — Converts an underscored string to CamelCase; pass `False` as the second argument to produce lowerCamelCase instead.

**dasherize(word)** — Replaces underscores with dashes in a string.

**humanize(word)** — Converts an underscored identifier to a human-readable string by replacing underscores with spaces, stripping a trailing `_id`, and capitalizing the first word.

**ordinal(number)** — Returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer, handling the 11/12/13 exceptions correctly.

**ordinalize(number)** — Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce strings like `"1st"` or `"42nd"`.

**parameterize(string, separator='-')** — Transliterates non-ASCII characters and replaces any remaining non-alphanumeric characters with a separator to produce a URL-safe slug.

**pluralize(word)** — Returns the plural form of an English word, respecting uncountable words and irregular forms.

**singularize(word)** — Returns the singular form of an English word, the reverse of `pluralize`, respecting uncountable words and irregular forms.

**tableize(word)** — Converts a class name or camelized string to a pluralized, underscored table name in the Rails convention.

**titleize(word)** — Capitalizes every word in a string and normalizes separators to produce a title-cased display string.

**transliterate(string)** — Replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII approximations using Unicode NFKD normalization, dropping characters with no approximation.

**underscore(word)** — Converts a CamelCase or mixed string to a lowercase, underscore-separated identifier; roughly the inverse of `camelize`.

## How the pieces fit together

The pluralization and singularization functions are driven by the module-level `PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`, and `UNCOUNTABLES` rule tables, which are extended at import time by `_irregular` calls for a set of known irregular English words; the remaining functions are independent string transformations that compose with each other (for example, `tableize` calls `underscore` then `pluralize`, and `titleize` calls `underscore` then `humanize`).

# inflection.py

A port of Ruby on Rails' string inflection library that transforms words between different naming conventions and grammatical forms.

## Public functions

**camelize** — converts an underscored string to CamelCase; when `uppercase_first_letter` is `False`, produces lowerCamelCase instead.

**dasherize** — replaces underscores with dashes in a string.

**humanize** — strips a trailing `_id`, replaces underscores with spaces, and capitalizes the first word, producing a readable label.

**ordinal** — returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer.

**ordinalize** — combines a number with its ordinal suffix into a single string (e.g., `1` → `"1st"`).

**parameterize** — transliterates non-ASCII characters and replaces remaining special characters with a separator (default `"-"`) to produce a URL-safe slug.

**pluralize** — returns the plural form of a word, respecting irregular forms and uncountable words.

**singularize** — returns the singular form of a word, the reverse of `pluralize`, respecting irregular forms and uncountable words.

**tableize** — converts a class-style name to a lowercase, underscored, pluralized table name (Rails convention).

**titleize** — capitalizes every word in a string after normalizing it, producing a title-cased label.

**transliterate** — replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII approximations using Unicode NFKD normalization, dropping characters with no approximation.

**underscore** — converts a CamelCase string to lowercase snake_case, the inverse of `camelize`.

## How the pieces fit together

The word-case converters (`camelize`, `underscore`, `dasherize`, `humanize`, `titleize`, `tableize`) form a pipeline of naming-convention transforms, while `pluralize`, `singularize`, `ordinal`, and `ordinalize` handle grammatical inflection — all backed by the `PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`, and `UNCOUNTABLES` rule tables that are pre-populated with irregular English words at module load time.

# inflection.py

Vendored from [jpvanhal/inflection @ 0.5.1](https://github.com/jpvanhal/inflection/tree/0.5.1). A port of the Ruby on Rails inflector to Python.

## What it does

The module transforms English words and identifier strings. It has two categories of functions:

**Word number (pluralization)**

- `pluralize(word)` — returns the plural form of a word.
- `singularize(word)` — returns the singular form of a word.

Both functions work by walking an ordered list of regex rules (`PLURALS` / `SINGULARS`) and returning the first match. Words in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (e.g. `fish`, `sheep`, `rice`) are returned unchanged. Irregular pairs (`person/people`, `child/children`, etc.) are registered at module load time via `_irregular()`, which prepends case-aware rules to both lists.

**Identifier / string casing**

- `camelize(string, uppercase_first_letter=True)` — `snake_case` → `CamelCase` (or `lowerCamelCase`).
- `underscore(word)` — `CamelCase` → `snake_case`. Roughly the inverse of `camelize`.
- `dasherize(word)` — replaces underscores with dashes.
- `humanize(word)` — `snake_case` → `"Human readable"` (strips trailing `_id`, capitalizes first word).
- `titleize(word)` — produces a title-cased string from any of the above forms.
- `tableize(word)` — Rails-style table name: `underscore` then `pluralize`.
- `parameterize(string, separator='-')` — URL-safe slug; runs `transliterate` first, then strips non-alphanumeric characters.
- `transliterate(string)` — drops or approximates non-ASCII characters via NFKD normalization.

**Ordinal numbers**

- `ordinal(number)` — returns the suffix for a number (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, `"th"`). Handles the 11/12/13 exceptions.
- `ordinalize(number)` — returns the number with its suffix appended (`1` → `"1st"`).

## Key data structures

| Name | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `PLURALS` | `list` of `(pattern, replacement)` | Ordered plural rules; checked top-to-bottom. |
| `SINGULARS` | `list` of `(pattern, replacement)` | Ordered singular rules; checked top-to-bottom. |
| `UNCOUNTABLES` | `set` of `str` | Words that have no plural/singular distinction. |

Rules are plain `re` patterns with case-insensitive flags embedded (`(?i)`). `_irregular()` inserts case-preserving rules at position 0 so they take priority over the general rules.

## Dependencies

Standard library only: `re`, `unicodedata`.

# inflection.py

A port of Ruby on Rails' string inflector to Python, providing functions to transform identifiers and words between common forms used in web frameworks and ORMs.

## Public functions

**camelize** — Converts an underscored string to CamelCase; pass `uppercase_first_letter=False` to produce lowerCamelCase instead of UpperCamelCase.

**dasherize** — Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen.

**humanize** — Strips a trailing `_id`, replaces underscores with spaces, lowercases the interior, and capitalizes the first letter, producing a readable label.

**ordinal** — Returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer, handling the special cases for 11–13.

**ordinalize** — Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a string such as `"1st"` or `"42nd"`.

**parameterize** — Transliterates non-ASCII characters, replaces disallowed characters with a separator (default `"-"`), collapses runs of the separator, strips leading/trailing separators, and lowercases the result, yielding a URL-safe slug.

**pluralize** — Returns the plural form of an English word by consulting the `UNCOUNTABLES` set first and then applying the ordered `PLURALS` rule list.

**singularize** — Returns the singular form of an English word by consulting `UNCOUNTABLES` first and then applying the ordered `SINGULARS` rule list; the inverse of `pluralize`.

**tableize** — Converts a class-style name to a database table name by underscoring it and then pluralizing the result.

**titleize** — Capitalizes every word in a string after normalizing it through `humanize` and `underscore`, suitable for display headings.

**transliterate** — Replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII approximation using Unicode NFKD decomposition, discarding characters that have no approximation.

**underscore** — Converts a CamelCase or mixed string to `snake_case`; the inverse of `camelize` for most inputs.

## How the pieces fit together

The pluralization and singularization functions are the core of the module, driven by the module-level `PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`, and `UNCOUNTABLES` tables (pre-populated with irregular forms via `_irregular` at import time), while the remaining functions handle identifier and string formatting transformations that often compose with one another—for example, `tableize` chains `underscore` into `pluralize`, and `titleize` chains `underscore` into `humanize`.

# inflection.py

A port of Ruby on Rails' inflector to Python, providing string transformation utilities for pluralization, singularization, and case conversion.

## Public Functions

**camelize(string, uppercase_first_letter=True)** — Converts an underscored string to CamelCase; produces UpperCamelCase by default, lowerCamelCase when `uppercase_first_letter=False`.

**dasherize(word)** — Replaces underscores with dashes in the string.

**humanize(word)** — Capitalizes the first word, replaces underscores with spaces, and strips a trailing `_id` suffix; intended for readable display output.

**ordinal(number)** — Returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer, handling the 11/12/13 exceptions.

**ordinalize(number)** — Returns the full ordinal string (e.g., `"1st"`, `"2nd"`) by combining the number with the result of `ordinal`.

**parameterize(string, separator='-')** — Transliterates non-ASCII characters and replaces special characters with a separator to produce URL-safe slugs.

**pluralize(word)** — Returns the plural form of a word, consulting the `UNCOUNTABLES` set and the `PLURALS` rule list (including irregular forms).

**singularize(word)** — Returns the singular form of a word, the reverse of `pluralize`, using the `SINGULARS` rule list and the `UNCOUNTABLES` set.

**tableize(word)** — Converts a class-style name to a lowercase, underscored, pluralized table name (e.g., Rails-style model-to-table mapping).

**titleize(word)** — Capitalizes every word in a string, replacing separators and CamelCase boundaries to produce a pretty title.

**transliterate(string)** — Replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII approximations using Unicode NFKD normalization; characters with no approximation are dropped.

**underscore(word)** — Converts a CamelCase string to a lowercase, underscore-separated form; approximate inverse of `camelize`.

## How the pieces fit together

The pluralization and singularization functions are driven by the `PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`, and `UNCOUNTABLES` module-level data structures, which are pre-populated with irregular word pairs at import time; all other functions are independent string transformations that compose with each other (e.g., `tableize` calls `underscore` then `pluralize`, and `titleize` calls `underscore` then `humanize`).

# inflection.py

A Python port of the string inflection logic from Ruby on Rails (originally by Janne Vanhala, v0.5.1, MIT license). Vendored from [jpvanhal/inflection @ 0.5.1](https://github.com/jpvanhal/inflection/tree/0.5.1).

## What it does

The module transforms English words and identifiers between common forms used in web frameworks and ORMs.

## Public functions

| Function | Input → Output | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `pluralize(word)` | singular → plural | `"octopus"` → `"octopi"` |
| `singularize(word)` | plural → singular | `"octopi"` → `"octopus"` |
| `camelize(string, uppercase_first_letter=True)` | underscore_case → CamelCase or lowerCamelCase | `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"` |
| `underscore(word)` | CamelCase → underscore_case | `"DeviceType"` → `"device_type"` |
| `dasherize(word)` | underscores → dashes | `"puni_puni"` → `"puni-puni"` |
| `humanize(word)` | underscore_case → readable label | `"author_id"` → `"Author"` |
| `titleize(word)` | any form → Title Case | `"man from the boondocks"` → `"Man From The Boondocks"` |
| `tableize(word)` | class name → table name | `"RawScaledScorer"` → `"raw_scaled_scorers"` |
| `ordinalize(number)` | integer → ordinal string | `1` → `"1st"`, `2` → `"2nd"` |
| `ordinal(number)` | integer → suffix only | `1` → `"st"`, `11` → `"th"` |
| `parameterize(string, separator='-')` | arbitrary string → URL slug | `"Donald E. Knuth"` → `"donald-e-knuth"` |
| `transliterate(string)` | unicode → ASCII approximation | `"älämölö"` → `"alamolo"` |

## How pluralization works

`pluralize` and `singularize` work through two mechanisms:

1. **Uncountables** — a hardcoded set (`UNCOUNTABLES`) of words like `"sheep"`, `"fish"`, and `"information"` that are returned unchanged.
2. **Rule lists** — ordered lists of `(regex_pattern, replacement)` tuples (`PLURALS` and `SINGULARS`). The first matching rule wins, so more-specific rules appear earlier in the list.

Irregular pairs (e.g. `person/people`, `child/children`) are prepended to the front of both lists at module load time via `_irregular()`, which generates case-preserving regex variants for each pair.

## Relationships between functions

- `tableize` = `pluralize(underscore(word))`
- `titleize` uses `humanize` and `underscore` internally
- `ordinalize` = `str(number) + ordinal(number)`
- `parameterize` calls `transliterate` first to handle non-ASCII input

## Notes on edge cases

- `camelize` and `underscore` are approximate inverses; round-tripping is not guaranteed for all inputs (e.g. `"IOError"` → `"io_error"` → `"IoError"`).
- `ordinal` takes the absolute value of its input, so negative numbers get the same suffix as their positive counterpart (e.g. `-1021` → `"st"`).
- All pluralization/singularization rules use `(?i)` case-insensitive matching, so `"CamelOctopus"` → `"CamelOctopi"` works correctly.

A port of Ruby on Rails' string inflection utilities to Python, providing functions for transforming words and identifiers between different grammatical and naming forms.

## Public functions

**camelize** — Converts an underscored string to CamelCase; pass `uppercase_first_letter=False` to produce lowerCamelCase instead of UpperCamelCase.

**dasherize** — Replaces underscores with dashes in a string.

**humanize** — Turns an underscored or machine-style string into a human-readable sentence-case phrase, stripping a trailing `_id` suffix if present.

**ordinal** — Returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for a given integer.

**ordinalize** — Returns a number combined with its ordinal suffix as a string (e.g., `1` → `"1st"`).

**parameterize** — Converts a string to a URL-safe slug by transliterating non-ASCII characters, replacing disallowed characters with a separator (default `-`), and lowercasing the result.

**pluralize** — Returns the plural form of a word using the `PLURALS` rule list, returning the word unchanged if it is uncountable.

**singularize** — Returns the singular form of a word using the `SINGULARS` rule list, returning the word unchanged if it is uncountable.

**tableize** — Converts a class or CamelCase name to a pluralized, underscored table name in the Rails convention.

**titleize** — Capitalizes every word in a string and normalizes separators and casing to produce a title-case display string.

**transliterate** — Replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII approximations via Unicode NFKD normalization, dropping characters with no approximation.

**underscore** — Converts a CamelCase or mixed string to a lowercase, underscore-separated form; broadly the inverse of `camelize`.

## How the pieces fit together

The pluralization and singularization functions are driven by module-level rule tables (`PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`, `UNCOUNTABLES`) that are pre-populated at import time — including irregular forms registered via the private `_irregular` helper — so that composed functions like `tableize` (which calls `underscore` then `pluralize`) work correctly with no additional setup.

# inflection.py

A port of Ruby on Rails' string inflector to Python, providing functions to transform words between plural/singular forms and various naming conventions (camelCase, snake_case, etc.).

## Public functions

**camelize** — converts an underscored string to CamelCase; when `uppercase_first_letter=False`, produces lowerCamelCase instead.

**dasherize** — replaces underscores with dashes in a string.

**humanize** — strips a trailing `_id`, replaces underscores with spaces, and capitalizes the first word; intended for readable display output.

**ordinal** — returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer.

**ordinalize** — returns the full ordinal string for a number (e.g. `1` → `"1st"`); combines the number and the result of `ordinal`.

**parameterize** — replaces special and non-ASCII characters in a string so it can be used as a URL slug, joining tokens with a configurable separator (default `"-"`).

**pluralize** — returns the plural form of a word using the `PLURALS` rule list and the `UNCOUNTABLES` set; leaves uncountable words unchanged.

**singularize** — returns the singular form of a word using the `SINGULARS` rule list and the `UNCOUNTABLES` set; the inverse of `pluralize`.

**tableize** — converts a class-style name to a plural snake_case table name by combining `underscore` and `pluralize`.

**titleize** — capitalizes every word in a string and normalizes separators and casing for use as a display title.

**transliterate** — replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII equivalents using Unicode NFKD normalization, dropping characters with no approximation.

**underscore** — converts a CamelCase or dasherized string to snake_case lowercase; the inverse of `camelize`.

## How the pieces fit together

The plural/singular functions are driven by ordered regex rule tables (`PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`) and a set of uncountable words (`UNCOUNTABLES`), while the case-conversion functions (`camelize`, `underscore`, `dasherize`, `humanize`, `titleize`, `tableize`) compose one another to cover the full range of Rails-style naming transformations.

# inflection.py

A port of Ruby on Rails' string inflector to Python, providing functions to pluralize, singularize, and transform identifier strings for display or database use.

## Public functions

**camelize(string, uppercase_first_letter=True)** — Converts an underscored string to CamelCase; produces UpperCamelCase by default and lowerCamelCase when `uppercase_first_letter` is `False`.

**dasherize(word)** — Replaces underscores with dashes in the string.

**humanize(word)** — Strips a trailing `_id`, replaces underscores with spaces, and capitalizes the first letter, producing a readable label from a raw identifier.

**ordinal(number)** — Returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for the given integer, handling the 11/12/13 edge cases correctly.

**ordinalize(number)** — Returns the number concatenated with its ordinal suffix (e.g., `1` → `"1st"`).

**parameterize(string, separator='-')** — Transliterates non-ASCII characters, replaces everything that is not alphanumeric with the separator, and lowercases the result, producing a URL-safe slug.

**pluralize(word)** — Returns the plural form of an English word, respecting uncountable words and irregular forms.

**singularize(word)** — Returns the singular form of an English word, the inverse of `pluralize`, respecting uncountable words and irregular forms.

**tableize(word)** — Converts a class-style name to a lowercase, underscored, pluralized table name (e.g., `"RawScaledScorer"` → `"raw_scaled_scorers"`).

**titleize(word)** — Capitalizes every word after normalizing underscores and CamelCase, producing a human-readable title.

**transliterate(string)** — Replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII equivalents via Unicode NFKD decomposition, dropping characters with no approximation.

**underscore(word)** — Converts a CamelCase or mixed string to lowercase with underscores, the rough inverse of `camelize`.

## How the pieces fit together

The pluralization and singularization functions are driven by the module-level `PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`, and `UNCOUNTABLES` rule tables, which are extended at import time with irregular-word entries via the private `_irregular` helper; the remaining functions are independent string transformations that `tableize` and `titleize` compose to build higher-level conversions.

# inflection.py

A port of Ruby on Rails' string inflector to Python, providing functions to transform words between plural/singular forms and various naming conventions.

## Public Functions

**camelize** — Converts an underscored string to CamelCase; pass `uppercase_first_letter=False` to get lowerCamelCase instead.

**dasherize** — Replaces underscores with dashes in a string.

**humanize** — Converts an underscored string to a human-readable form by replacing underscores with spaces, stripping a trailing `_id`, and capitalizing the first word.

**ordinal** — Returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer.

**ordinalize** — Returns the full ordinal string for a given integer (e.g., `1` → `"1st"`).

**parameterize** — Transliterates and sanitizes a string for use in a URL slug, replacing non-alphanumeric characters with a separator (default `-`).

**pluralize** — Returns the plural form of an English word, respecting irregular forms and uncountable nouns.

**singularize** — Returns the singular form of an English word, the reverse of `pluralize`.

**tableize** — Converts a class name or camelCase string to a pluralized, underscored table name (Rails-style).

**titleize** — Capitalizes every word in a string and cleans up separators to produce a title-cased display string.

**transliterate** — Replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII equivalents, dropping any that have no approximation.

**underscore** — Converts a CamelCase or dasherized string to `snake_case` lowercase form.

The plural/singular functions are driven by two ordered rule tables (`PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`) and a set of uncountable words (`UNCOUNTABLES`), with irregular pairs (e.g., person/people) prepended at module load time via the private `_irregular` helper.

# inflection.py

Vendored from [jpvanhal/inflection @ 0.5.1](https://github.com/jpvanhal/inflection/tree/0.5.1). A port of the Ruby on Rails string inflector to Python. No third-party dependencies; uses only `re` and `unicodedata` from the standard library.

## What it does

The module transforms strings between naming conventions and grammatical forms. All public functions take a string (or int for ordinal functions) and return a string.

### Plural / singular

- `pluralize(word)` — returns the plural form of an English word.
- `singularize(word)` — returns the singular form.

Both work by walking an ordered list of regex rules (`PLURALS` / `SINGULARS`) and returning the first match. Words in `UNCOUNTABLES` (e.g. `"sheep"`, `"fish"`, `"information"`) are returned unchanged.

Irregular words (person/people, man/men, child/children, cow/kine, etc.) are registered at module load time via `_irregular()`, which prepends case-preserving rules to the front of both lists.

### Case / separator transformations

| Function | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|
| `camelize(s, uppercase_first_letter=True)` | `"device_type"` | `"DeviceType"` / `"deviceType"` |
| `underscore(s)` | `"DeviceType"` | `"device_type"` |
| `dasherize(s)` | `"puni_puni"` | `"puni-puni"` |
| `titleize(s)` | `"man from the boondocks"` | `"Man From The Boondocks"` |
| `humanize(s)` | `"employee_salary"` | `"Employee salary"` |
| `tableize(s)` | `"RawScaledScorer"` | `"raw_scaled_scorers"` |
| `parameterize(s, separator='-')` | `"Donald E. Knuth"` | `"donald-e-knuth"` |

`tableize` is a composition: `pluralize(underscore(word))`.

`titleize` is also composed: it calls `humanize(underscore(word))` then capitalizes each word boundary.

`parameterize` calls `transliterate` first to drop non-ASCII characters, then replaces anything that isn't alphanumeric or `-`/`_` with the separator.

### Ordinals

- `ordinal(number)` — returns the suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a number.
- `ordinalize(number)` — returns the number with its suffix (e.g. `"1st"`, `"42nd"`).

The teen numbers 11–13 always use `"th"` regardless of their last digit.

### Unicode helper

- `transliterate(string)` — strips or approximates non-ASCII characters using NFKD normalization followed by ASCII encoding with `ignore`. Used internally by `parameterize`.

## Data tables

`PLURALS` and `SINGULARS` are module-level lists of `(pattern, replacement)` tuples applied in order. `_irregular()` mutates these lists at import time by inserting high-priority rules at index 0, so irregular forms always win over the general rules.

`UNCOUNTABLES` is a `set` for O(1) membership tests.

# inflection.py

A port of Ruby on Rails' string inflector to Python, providing functions for transforming words between plural, singular, and various identifier and display formats.

## Public functions

**camelize** — Converts an underscored string to CamelCase; when `uppercase_first_letter` is `False`, produces lowerCamelCase instead.

**dasherize** — Replaces underscores with dashes in a string.

**humanize** — Strips a trailing `_id`, replaces underscores with spaces, and capitalizes the first word, producing a readable label from an identifier.

**ordinal** — Returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for a given integer, handling the 11/12/13 exceptions.

**ordinalize** — Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a string such as `"1st"` or `"42nd"`.

**parameterize** — Transliterates non-ASCII characters and replaces all remaining non-alphanumeric characters with a separator (default `"-"`), producing a URL-safe slug.

**pluralize** — Returns the plural form of an English word using the `PLURALS` rule list, leaving uncountable words unchanged.

**singularize** — Returns the singular form of an English word using the `SINGULARS` rule list, leaving uncountable words unchanged.

**tableize** — Converts a class name or mixed-case string to a lowercase, underscored, pluralized table name (equivalent to `pluralize(underscore(word))`).

**titleize** — Capitalizes every word in a string after normalizing it through `humanize` and `underscore`, producing a title-cased display string.

**transliterate** — Replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII approximation via Unicode NFKD normalization, dropping characters that have no approximation.

**underscore** — Converts a CamelCase or mixed string to a lowercase, underscore-separated identifier; broadly the inverse of `camelize`.

## How the pieces fit together

The pluralization and singularization functions are driven by ordered rule tables (`PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`) and an uncountable set, both of which are populated at import time with built-in patterns and irregular-word entries; the remaining functions are independent string transformers that compose together (for example, `tableize` calls `underscore` then `pluralize`, and `titleize` calls `underscore` then `humanize`) to cover the full range of identifier and display-string conversions.

A port of Ruby on Rails' string inflection library, providing functions to transform words between plural/singular forms, naming conventions, and URL-safe representations.

**camelize** — converts an underscored string to CamelCase; pass `uppercase_first_letter=False` for lowerCamelCase.

**dasherize** — replaces underscores with dashes in a string.

**humanize** — turns an underscored string into a human-readable sentence-case phrase, stripping a trailing `_id` if present.

**ordinal** — returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer.

**ordinalize** — returns the full ordinal string for a number (e.g., `1` → `"1st"`).

**parameterize** — replaces special and non-ASCII characters in a string to produce a URL-slug, using a configurable separator (default `"-"`).

**pluralize** — returns the plural form of an English word, respecting irregular forms and uncountable nouns.

**singularize** — returns the singular form of an English word, the reverse of `pluralize`.

**tableize** — converts a class-style name to a pluralized, underscored table name (e.g., `"RawScaledScorer"` → `"raw_scaled_scorers"`).

**titleize** — capitalizes every word in a string and normalizes separators to produce a pretty title.

**transliterate** — replaces non-ASCII characters with their closest ASCII equivalents, discarding any that have none.

**underscore** — converts a CamelCase string to lowercase snake_case, the inverse of `camelize`.

The pluralization and singularization functions are driven by the module-level `PLURALS`, `SINGULARS`, and `UNCOUNTABLES` rule tables, which are pre-populated at import time with irregular word pairs via the private `_irregular` helper.

# inflection.py

A Python port of the Ruby on Rails string inflector, vendored from
[jpvanhal/inflection @ 0.5.1](https://github.com/jpvanhal/inflection/tree/0.5.1).

## Purpose

Transforms strings between common naming conventions and grammatical forms used
in web frameworks: plural/singular, CamelCase/snake_case, human-readable labels,
URL slugs, and ordinal numbers.

## Public API

| Function | Input | Output | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pluralize(word)` | singular or plural noun | plural form | `"octopus"` → `"octopi"` |
| `singularize(word)` | plural or singular noun | singular form | `"posts"` → `"post"` |
| `camelize(string, uppercase_first_letter=True)` | underscore string | CamelCase | `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"` |
| `underscore(word)` | CamelCase string | snake_case | `"DeviceType"` → `"device_type"` |
| `dasherize(word)` | underscore string | dash-separated | `"puni_puni"` → `"puni-puni"` |
| `humanize(word)` | underscore string | readable label | `"author_id"` → `"Author"` |
| `titleize(word)` | any string | Title Case | `"man from the boondocks"` → `"Man From The Boondocks"` |
| `tableize(word)` | class name | table name | `"RawScaledScorer"` → `"raw_scaled_scorers"` |
| `parameterize(string, separator='-')` | arbitrary string | URL slug | `"Donald E. Knuth"` → `"donald-e-knuth"` |
| `transliterate(string)` | unicode string | ASCII approximation | `"älämölö"` → `"alamolo"` |
| `ordinal(number)` | integer | suffix string | `1` → `"st"`, `2` → `"nd"` |
| `ordinalize(number)` | integer | ordinal string | `1` → `"1st"`, `11` → `"11th"` |

## Data structures

**`PLURALS`** and **`SINGULARS`** are ordered lists of `(pattern, replacement)` regex
pairs. Each function walks the list and applies the first matching rule, so more
specific rules must appear before more general ones. The module-level
`_irregular()` calls at the bottom of the file prepend exception rules for words
like `person/people` and `child/children`.

**`UNCOUNTABLES`** is a set of words that have no plural/singular distinction
(`fish`, `sheep`, `equipment`, etc.). Both `pluralize` and `singularize` return
the word unchanged if it appears in this set.

## Notable behaviors

- All regex patterns use `(?i)` case-insensitive flags, so `"CamelOctopus"` →
  `"CamelOctopi"` works correctly.
- `camelize` and `underscore` are approximate inverses; round-tripping through
  both can alter capitalization (e.g. `"IOError"` → `"io_error"` → `"IoError"`).
- `parameterize` calls `transliterate` first, so accented characters are
  converted to ASCII before non-alphanumeric characters are replaced with the
  separator.
- `ordinal` handles the 11/12/13 edge case (always `"th"`) before the ones-digit
  lookup, so `11` → `"th"` rather than `"st"`.
